texts.title: The Book of Various Heresies, Chapters 29–43: The Gnostic Schools
texts.critical_edition: Marx, F. (ed.), Corpus Scriptorum Ecclesiasticorum Latinorum 38, Vienna: Tempsky, 1898
texts.description: The first English translation of any part of Filastrius’s catalog of heresies. These fifteen chapters cover every major Gnostic school known to the late fourth-century Church: the Simonians, Basilideans, Valentinians, Marcosians, Carpocratians, and the Barbelo-Gnostics.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet XI: The Flood
texts.critical_edition: George, A. R. (ed.), The Babylonian Gilgamesh Epic, Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2003
texts.description: The oldest flood narrative in world literature, predating Genesis by over a millennium. Utnapishtim tells Gilgamesh how the gods destroyed humanity with a great flood, and how he alone was granted immortality. Contains the earliest meditation on human mortality and the question of whether divinity can be shared with mortals.

texts.title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The Prologue of John's Gospel from the earliest complete New Testament manuscript. The foundational text for Logos theology and the starting point for the theosis debate. Contains the key variant: Codex Sinaiticus reads 'only-begotten God' (monogenes theos) where later manuscripts read 'only-begotten Son.'

texts.title: Gospel of John 10:22–39: “I Said, You Are Gods”
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The most direct confrontation in the Gospels over Christ's divinity. Jesus declares 'I and the Father are one,' is accused of blasphemy, and responds by quoting Psalm 82: 'I said, you are gods.' The single most contested passage in the theosis debate.

texts.title: 2 Peter 1:3–4: “Partakers of the Divine Nature”
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The most explicit theosis statement in the New Testament: 'that you may become partakers of the divine nature.' The scriptural foundation for the entire Eastern Orthodox doctrine of divinization.

texts.title: On the Incarnation, Chapter 54: “He Became Man That We Might Become God”
texts.critical_edition: Thomson, R. W. (ed.), Athanasius: Contra Gentes and De Incarnatione, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1971
texts.description: The most famous sentence in the history of the theosis doctrine. Athanasius argues that the entire purpose of the Incarnation was not merely to save humanity from sin but to transform human nature: to make the mortal immortal, the human divine.

texts.title: Gospel of John 17:20-26: The High Priestly Prayer
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: Jesus's prayer for all future believers: "that they all may be one, just as you, Father, are in me and I in you, that they also may be in us." The most explicit statement of mutual indwelling in the New Testament, extending the divine-human unity to all believers.

texts.title: Gospel of Luke 1:1-4: The Historian's Prologue
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: Luke's prologue is the earliest statement of historical methodology in Christian literature. He tells Theophilus he has investigated everything carefully from eyewitnesses — a statement of source verification that mirrors the mission of this library.

texts.title: Gospel of Mark 1:1-11: The Beginning and the Baptism
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The opening of the earliest Gospel. No birth narrative — straight to John the Baptist and the baptism, where the heavens are torn open, the Spirit descends, and the voice declares: "You are my beloved Son." The moment the divine enters the human.

texts.title: Gospel of Matthew 5:43-48: "Be Perfect As Your Father Is Perfect"
texts.source: Codex Sinaiticus (4th century), British Library Add. MS 43725
texts.critical_edition: Lake, K. and Lake, S. (eds.), Codex Sinaiticus Petropolitanus, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1911
texts.description: The climax of the Sermon on the Mount's ethical teaching. "Be perfect (teleioi) as your heavenly Father is perfect." The word teleios means complete, whole — reaching the telos. For the theosis tradition, this is a command to become like God.

texts.title: Refutation of All Heresies, Book 6, Chapters 24–37
texts.critical_edition: Marcovich, M. (ed.), Hippolytus: Refutatio Omnium Haeresium, Berlin: De Gruyter, 1986
texts.description: Hippolytus describes Valentinian Gnostic theology in detail: the divine Pleroma, the emanation of aeons, the fall of Sophia, the creation of the material world by the Demiurge, and the divine spark trapped within humanity.

texts.title: Against Heresies, Book III, Chapter 19: "He Became What We Are"
texts.critical_edition: Rousseau, A. and Doutreleau, L. (eds.), Sources Chrétiennes 211, Paris: Cerf, 1974
texts.description: The earliest systematic articulation of the theosis doctrine. Irenaeus argues that the Word became what we are so that we might become what he is.

texts.title: Against the Valentinians
texts.critical_edition: Fredouille, J.-C. (ed.), Sources Chrétiennes 280–281, Paris: Cerf, 1980
texts.description: Tertullian’s sharp, satirical attack on Valentinian Gnosticism. Written entirely in Latin by one of the most vivid prose stylists in Christian history.

texts.title: Against Arius, Book 1
texts.critical_edition: Henry, P. and Hadot, P. (eds.), Sources Chrétiennes 68, Paris: Cerf, 1960
texts.description: A Neoplatonist philosopher who converted to Christianity in old age, Marius Victorinus brought the full apparatus of Greek philosophical thought to bear on the question of Christ’s divinity.

texts.title: Psalm 82: "I Said, You Are Gods"
texts.critical_edition: Rahlfs, A. (ed.), Septuaginta, Stuttgart: Deutsche Bibelgesellschaft, 2006
texts.description: The psalm Jesus quotes at John 10:34. God stands in the divine assembly, judges the gods, and declares: "I said, you are gods." The most important Old Testament text for the theosis debate.

texts.title: Genesis 1:26-28: "Let Us Make Man in Our Image"
texts.critical_edition: Rahlfs, A. (ed.), Septuaginta, Stuttgart: Deutsche Bibelgesellschaft, 2006
texts.description: The creation of humanity in the image (eikon) and likeness (homoiosis) of God. The distinction between image and likeness became central to the theosis tradition: image is given at creation, likeness is achieved through divinization.

texts.title: The Nicene Creed (Niceno-Constantinopolitan, 381 AD)
texts.source: Acts of the Council of Constantinople (381 AD)
texts.critical_edition: Denzinger, H. (ed.), Enchiridion Symbolorum, 43rd ed., San Francisco: Ignatius Press, 2012
texts.description: The formal resolution of the first great theological crisis. Defines Christ as homoousios (of one substance) with the Father. For the theosis tradition, the Creed is both foundation and constraint.

texts.title: Romans 8:29-30: Conformed to the Image of His Son
texts.description: Predestined to be symmorphous (conformed) to the image of the Son. The divine plan includes human transformation into Christ's likeness.

texts.title: Galatians 2:20: Christ Lives in Me
texts.description: It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me. The most personal statement of mutual indwelling in Paul.

texts.title: Colossians 1:15-20: The Christ Hymn
texts.description: Christ as the image of the invisible God, firstborn of all creation, in whom all the fullness (pleroma) was pleased to dwell.

texts.title: Philippians 2:5-11: The Kenosis Hymn
texts.description: Though in the form (morphe) of God, he emptied himself. The descent-ascent pattern: God descends to humanity so humanity can be exalted.

texts.title: 1 John 3:1-3: We Shall Be Like Him
texts.description: When he appears we shall be like him (homoioi auto), for we shall see him as he is. The eschatological promise of transformation through vision.

texts.title: Ephesians 4:11-16: The Measure of the Fullness of Christ
texts.description: Until we all attain to the measure of the stature of the fullness (pleroma) of Christ. Maturity as participation in divine fullness.

texts.title: Hebrews 1:1-4: The Radiance of God's Glory
texts.description: The Son is the radiance (apaugasma) of God's glory and the exact imprint (charakter) of his nature (hypostasis).

texts.title: 1 Corinthians 15:42-49: The Image of the Heavenly
texts.description: As we have borne the image of the man of dust, we shall also bear the image of the man of heaven. Bodily transformation, not just spiritual.

texts.title: Galatians 4:4-7: Adoption as Sons
texts.description: God sent his Son so that we might receive adoption (huiothesia) as sons. The Spirit of the Son in our hearts, crying Abba, Father.

texts.title: Romans 8:14-17: Heirs of God, Co-Heirs with Christ
texts.description: If children, then heirs: heirs of God and co-heirs (synkleronomoi) with Christ. Shared inheritance implies shared nature.

texts.title: Exodus 3:13-15: I AM WHO I AM
texts.description: The divine name revealed to Moses. Ego eimi ho on. The ontological claim that defines the God of Israel.

texts.title: Isaiah 6:1-8: The Throne Vision
texts.description: Holy, holy, holy. The seraphim, the coal that purifies lips, the call to prophecy. The paradigmatic encounter with divine glory.

texts.title: Ezekiel 1:26-28: The Divine Throne-Chariot
texts.description: Upon the likeness of a throne, the likeness of the appearance of a man. The glory of the Lord. Foundation of the merkabah tradition.

texts.title: Daniel 7:13-14: One Like a Son of Man
texts.description: One like a son of man, coming with the clouds of heaven, receiving everlasting dominion. Jesus's preferred self-designation.

texts.title: Proverbs 8:22-31: Wisdom Before Creation
texts.description: The Lord created/possessed me at the beginning. Wisdom (Sophia) as pre-existent divine agent. Arians said created; Nicenes said begotten.

texts.title: Ecclesiastes 3:10-11: Eternity in the Heart
texts.description: He has put eternity (aion/olam) into the human heart. The claim that something infinite is placed within mortal humanity.

texts.description: Protrepticus 1.8.4: The Word Became Man So You May Learn How Man Becomes God

texts.title: Zhu Xi Commentary 1
texts.description: Zhu Xi Commentary 1

texts.title: Fragments: All Things Are One
texts.description: The surviving fragments of Heraclitus on the Logos, the unity of opposites, and the ever-living fire.

texts.title: On Nature: The Way of Truth
texts.description: Parmenides' poem on Being: what is, is; what is not, cannot be thought or spoken.

texts.title: Purifications: The Fallen Daimon
texts.description: Empedocles on the divine soul exiled into mortal flesh and the cosmic cycles of Love and Strife.

texts.title: The Boundless: The Oldest Philosophical Fragment
texts.description: The single surviving fragment of Anaximander — the oldest known philosophical text in Western history.

texts.title: Fragments on Nous: Mind Orders All Things
texts.description: Anaxagoras on Mind (Nous) as the cosmic ordering principle that set the universe in motion.

texts.title: The Paradoxes of Motion
texts.description: Zeno's famous paradoxes — Achilles and the Tortoise, the Dichotomy, the Arrow — challenging the reality of motion and plurality.

texts.title: Iliad, Book I: The Wrath of Achilles
texts.description: The opening of the Iliad: Achilles' rage, Agamemnon's hubris, and the gods' intervention in mortal affairs.

texts.title: Odyssey, Book I: The Counsel of the Gods
texts.description: The opening of the Odyssey: the gods debate Odysseus' fate, Athena descends to Ithaca.

texts.title: Odyssey, Book XI: The Descent to the Dead
texts.description: Odysseus' nekuia — his journey to the land of the dead to consult the prophet Tiresias.

texts.title: Theogony: The Birth of the Gods
texts.description: Hesiod's account of creation: from Chaos to the reign of Zeus, the genealogy of all the gods.

texts.title: Works and Days: The Five Ages of Man
texts.description: Hesiod's account of humanity's decline from the Golden Age through Silver, Bronze, Heroic, and Iron Ages.

texts.title: Prometheus Bound: The Suffering of the Fire-Bringer
texts.description: Prometheus, chained to a rock for giving fire to humanity, defies Zeus and prophesies his fall.

texts.title: Antigone: The Ode to Man
texts.description: The chorus of Antigone sings the 'Ode to Man' — humanity as the most wondrous and terrible creature.

texts.title: Oedipus the King: Know Thyself
texts.description: The moment of recognition: Oedipus discovers he has killed his father and married his mother.

texts.title: The Bacchae: Dionysus Arrives
texts.description: Dionysus returns to Thebes disguised as a mortal; King Pentheus resists and is destroyed.

texts.title: Republic VII: The Allegory of the Cave
texts.description: Plato's most famous image: prisoners chained in a cave, mistaking shadows for reality.

texts.title: Timaeus: The Demiurge Creates the World
texts.description: Plato's creation myth: the divine craftsman fashions the cosmos as a living image of eternity.

texts.title: Metaphysics XII: The Unmoved Mover
texts.description: Aristotle's theology: God as pure thought thinking itself, the first cause that moves all things by being desired.

texts.title: Nicomachean Ethics X: The Contemplative Life
texts.description: Aristotle's argument that the highest human happiness is philosophical contemplation — becoming as godlike as possible.

texts.title: Fragments: Desire and the Divine
texts.description: The surviving fragments of Sappho — the poetry that ancient critics ranked alongside Homer.

texts.title: The Melian Dialogue: The Strong Do What They Can
texts.description: Athens demands the surrender of Melos: the most brutal statement of political realism ever written.

texts.title: Histories III.80-82: The Debate on Government
texts.description: Three Persian nobles debate democracy, oligarchy, and monarchy — the first recorded political theory.

texts.title: Pythian 8: Creatures of a Day
texts.description: Pindar's meditation on human mortality: 'What is someone? What is no one? A shadow's dream is man.'

texts.title: Fragments: The Hedgehog and the Fox
texts.description: The earliest Greek lyric poet — soldier, lover, realist — and the original source of 'the fox knows many things, the hedgehog one great thing.'

texts.title: Water Is the First Principle
texts.description: The earliest recorded philosophical proposition in Western history: all things are water.

texts.title: On Providence: The Stoic World-Soul
texts.description: Chrysippus on the rational soul of the cosmos, fate, and the identity of God with nature.

texts.title: Phaedrus: The Chariot of the Soul
texts.description: Plato's image of the soul as a charioteer driving two winged horses — one noble, one base.

texts.title: Atharva Veda 10.2: The Body as Cosmic City
texts.description: The human body as a city with eight wheels and nine gates, containing the divine.

texts.title: Rig Veda 1.1: Hymn to Agni
texts.description: The opening hymn of the Rig Veda — the oldest text in any Indo-European language.

texts.title: Pyramid Texts, Utterance 217: The King Ascends to Heaven
texts.description: The oldest religious texts in the world — spells for the pharaoh's ascent to the stars.

texts.title: Coffin Texts, Spell 335: I Am Atum
texts.description: The democratization of the afterlife — any person, not just the pharaoh, can become one with the creator god.

texts.title: Enki and the World Order
texts.description: The Sumerian creation myth: Enki assigns every god, city, and element its proper function in the cosmos.

texts.title: Lament for the Destruction of Ur
texts.description: One of the oldest pieces of literature: a city mourns its destruction and begs the gods for mercy.

texts.title: Meditations II: Impermanence
texts.description: The emperor-philosopher on the brevity of life, the smallness of human affairs, and the duty to act justly regardless.

texts.title: Moral Epistles 65: The Five Causes
texts.description: Seneca synthesizes Plato, Aristotle, and the Stoics on the causes of existence — matter, form, agent, purpose, and the Idea.

texts.title: Dhammapada 11-12: Old Age
texts.description: The Buddha on aging, death, and the urgency of awakening while there is still time.

texts.title: Mencius 2A.6: The Four Beginnings
texts.description: Mencius' argument that human nature is inherently good — the four innate moral feelings.

texts.title: Zhuangzi 2: The Equality of Things
texts.description: Zhuangzi's radical relativism: all distinctions — right/wrong, self/other, life/death — dissolve in the Tao.

texts.title: Ecclesiastes 1: Vanity of Vanities
texts.description: The preacher's devastating meditation on the meaninglessness of human striving under the sun.

texts.title: Job 38: The Voice from the Whirlwind
texts.description: God answers Job out of the whirlwind — not with explanations but with questions about the foundations of the cosmos.

texts.title: 4 Ezra 7: The Few and the Many
texts.description: A Jewish apocalypse written after the destruction of the Temple: why does God save so few?

texts.title: Voluspa 1-8: The Creation of the World
texts.description: The Norse creation myth: from the void of Ginnungagap to the making of the world from Ymir's body.

texts.title: Havamal 1-20: The Wisdom of the High One
texts.description: Practical wisdom attributed to Odin: hospitality, caution, the proper way to live among others.

texts.title: Yasna 44: Questions to Ahura Mazda
texts.description: Zoroaster questions Ahura Mazda about the nature of creation, truth, and cosmic order.

texts.title: Gospel of Philip: The Bridal Chamber
texts.description: A Valentinian sacramental text on the mystery of spiritual marriage and the restoration of unity.

texts.title: Fragments: Justice and the City
texts.description: Solon the lawgiver of Athens on justice, wealth, hubris, and the proper ordering of the city.

texts.title: Fragments: The Ship of State
texts.description: Alcaeus of Mytilene: the poet who gave Western literature the metaphor of the ship of state.

texts.title: Elegies: Trust No One
texts.description: Theognis of Megara on the corruption of values, the unreliability of friends, and survival in a declining age.

texts.title: Tain Bo Cuailnge: The Cattle Raid of Cooley
texts.source: Book of the Dun Cow (Lebor na hUidre)
texts.description: The Irish Iliad: Cuchulainn alone defends Ulster against the armies of Connacht.

texts.title: Lebor Gabala Erenn: The Book of Invasions
texts.source: Book of Leinster; Book of Ballymote
texts.description: The mythological history of Ireland: five successive invasions from the creation of the world to the coming of the Gaels.

texts.title: The Mabinogion: Pwyll Prince of Dyfed
texts.source: White Book of Rhydderch; Red Book of Hergest
texts.description: The first branch of the Mabinogi: Pwyll's journey to the Otherworld and his encounter with Rhiannon.

texts.title: Gallic War VI.13-18: The Druids and Celtic Religion
texts.description: Caesar's account of the Druids: their doctrine of the immortality of the soul, their authority, and their human sacrifices.

texts.title: The Song of Amergin
texts.source: Lebor Gabala Erenn; Book of Leinster
texts.description: The oldest poem in Irish literature: the druid Amergin claims identity with every element of creation.

texts.title: Kojiki: The Creation of Japan
texts.description: The oldest surviving Japanese text: the creation of the islands from the divine couple Izanagi and Izanami.

texts.title: Nihon Shoki: The Age of the Gods
texts.description: Japan's official chronicle: the age of the gods, the sun goddess Amaterasu, and the divine origin of the emperor.

texts.title: The Tale of the Heike: The Bells of Gion
texts.description: The Japanese Iliad: the rise and fall of the Taira clan, opening with the most famous lines in Japanese literature.

texts.title: The Pillow Book: In Spring, the Dawn
texts.description: The world's first great work of personal essay: Sei Shonagon's observations on beauty, nature, and court life in Heian Japan.

texts.title: Hojoki: An Account of My Hut
texts.description: A Buddhist meditation on impermanence after earthquake, fire, and famine: the original tiny-house manifesto.

texts.title: Bardo Thodol: Liberation Through Hearing in the Intermediate State
texts.description: The Tibetan Book of the Dead: instructions for consciousness navigating the states between death and rebirth.

texts.title: The Hundred Thousand Songs: Song of Realization
texts.description: Songs of awakening by Tibet's most beloved yogi — who went from murderer to enlightened master in a single lifetime.

texts.title: Popol Vuh: The Creation of Humanity
texts.description: The Mayan Genesis: the gods' repeated attempts to create beings who can worship them, culminating in humans made from corn.

texts.title: Legend of the Fifth Sun: Nanahuatzin's Sacrifice
texts.description: The Aztec creation myth: the humble god Nanahuatzin throws himself into the fire to become the sun.

texts.title: Chilam Balam of Chumayel: The Creation of the World
texts.description: Mayan prophetic and cosmological texts: the creation and ordering of the world through the four cardinal directions.

texts.title: Kebra Nagast: The Glory of Kings
texts.source: Ethiopian National Archives; British Library Or. 819
texts.description: Ethiopia's national epic: the Queen of Sheba, King Solomon, and the transfer of the Ark of the Covenant to Aksum.

texts.title: Ifa Corpus: The Creation of the World
texts.description: The Yoruba creation narrative: Olodumare sends Obatala to create dry land, and Orunmila brings wisdom to humanity.

texts.title: Odu Ifa: The Sixteen Sacred Chapters
texts.description: The core divination texts of the Yoruba: 256 chapters encoding the wisdom of Orunmila on fate, sacrifice, and right action.

texts.title: The Maxims of Ptahhotep
texts.description: The oldest complete book in the world: a vizier's instructions on justice, humility, and the art of listening.

texts.title: Russian Primary Chronicle: The Calling of the Varangians
texts.description: The foundation myth of Russia: the Slavic tribes invite the Varangian Rus to come and rule over them.

texts.title: The Slavic Creation: Rod and the World Egg
texts.description: The pre-Christian Slavic creation myth: the supreme god Rod hatches the cosmos from a golden egg.

texts.title: Shahnameh: The Creation of the World and Gayomart
texts.source: British Library Or. 12688; Florence Magl. III.24
texts.description: The Persian national epic: the creation of the world, the first man Gayomart, and the eternal struggle of good against evil.

texts.title: Masnavi: The Song of the Reed
texts.description: The opening of Rumi's spiritual epic: the reed flute's lament for separation from the reed bed — the soul's longing for God.

texts.title: Rubaiyat: The Quatrains
texts.description: Omar Khayyam's quatrains on wine, mortality, and the unknowability of the cosmos.

texts.title: Acaranga Sutra: The Book of Good Conduct
texts.description: The oldest surviving Jain text: Mahavira's teachings on non-violence, the suffering of all beings, and the ascetic path.

texts.title: The Baal Cycle: Baal Against the Sea
texts.description: The Canaanite creation myth: the storm god Baal defeats the sea god Yam and builds his palace on Mount Zaphon.

texts.title: The Baal Cycle: Baal Against Death
texts.description: Baal descends to the underworld, is swallowed by Death, and is restored to life by his sister Anat.

texts.title: Samguk Yusa: The Myth of Tangun
texts.description: Korea's foundation myth: the son of heaven descends to earth, a bear becomes a woman, and their child Tangun founds the nation.

texts.title: Reamker: The Cambodian Ramayana
texts.description: Cambodia's national epic: the story of Rama adapted through Khmer Buddhism and carved into the walls of Angkor Wat.

texts.title: Kakawin Ramayana: The Old Javanese Ramayana
texts.description: The oldest literary work in Javanese: the Ramayana reimagined through the lens of Javanese Hindu-Buddhist civilization.

texts.title: Kalevala: The Creation from the World Egg
texts.source: Finnish Literary Society (SKS); oral sources pre-1000 AD
texts.description: The Finnish national epic: the world is created from the shattered egg of a goldeneye duck on the knee of the water-mother.

texts.title: Deirdre of the Sorrows
texts.description: The most tragic love story in Irish literature: Deirdre's prophecy, her flight with Naoise, and their destruction by King Conchobar.

texts.title: The Wooing of Etain
texts.description: A woman is reborn three times across a thousand years, pursued by an immortal lover from the Otherworld.

texts.title: The Voyage of Bran
texts.description: Bran sails to the Land of Women beyond the sea, where there is no sorrow, sickness, or death.

texts.title: The Cattle Raid of Flidais
texts.description: The warrior-goddess Flidais feeds armies from her magical cattle — a tale of divine abundance and war.

texts.title: The Settling of the Manor of Tara
texts.description: The five provinces of Ireland correspond to the five elements and five directions — the Celtic cosmological map.

texts.title: The Dream of Oengus
texts.description: The god of love sees a woman in a dream and searches all Ireland and the Otherworld to find her.

texts.title: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired
texts.description: The Tuatha De Danann wage war against the Fomorians — the Irish Titanomachy.

texts.title: Oisin in the Land of Youth
texts.description: The warrior-poet Oisin rides to the Land of Youth with Niamh, returns 300 years later to find Ireland changed forever.

texts.title: The Cattle Raid of Fraech
texts.description: The most beautiful man in Ireland braves a monster-infested lake to win the woman he loves.

texts.title: The Colloquy of the Ancients
texts.description: The last surviving Fianna warriors meet St. Patrick and recount the tales of pagan Ireland.

texts.title: The Tale of Genji: The Paulownia Pavilion
texts.description: The world's first novel: the radiant Prince Genji, his forbidden love, and the beauty that suffuses a world of sorrow.

texts.title: The Narrow Road to the Interior
texts.description: Basho's masterpiece: a poetic pilgrimage through northern Japan, where every landscape holds a haiku.

texts.title: Tsurezuregusa: Essays in Idleness
texts.description: A monk's reflections on beauty, impermanence, and the art of living — the Japanese Montaigne.

texts.title: Atsumori: A Noh Play on War and Compassion
texts.description: A warrior who killed a boy in battle returns years later as a monk and meets the boy's ghost — forgiveness across death.

texts.title: Genjokoan: Actualizing the Fundamental Point
texts.description: Dogen's most famous essay: the nature of enlightenment, the self, and the moon reflected in water.

texts.title: Uji: Being-Time
texts.description: Dogen's radical teaching that time and existence are one and the same — each moment is all of being.

texts.title: Man'yoshu: Poems of a Myriad Leaves
texts.description: The oldest Japanese poetry anthology: 4,500 poems from emperors, soldiers, farmers, and anonymous lovers.

texts.title: Kokinwakashu: The Anthology of Ancient and Modern Poetry
texts.description: The first imperially commissioned poetry anthology: the kana preface defines Japanese aesthetics for a millennium.

texts.title: Kojiki: The Death of Yamato Takeru
texts.description: The tragic death of Japan's greatest hero — fatally wounded, he transforms into a white bird and flies toward heaven.

texts.title: Mahabharata: The Dice Game
texts.description: The fateful dice game where Yudhishthira gambles away his kingdom, his brothers, and his wife Draupadi.

texts.title: Mahabharata: Bhishma on the Bed of Arrows
texts.description: The dying Bhishma, pierced by a thousand arrows, delivers his final teachings on dharma, governance, and death.

texts.title: Ramayana: Rama's Exile to the Forest
texts.description: Rama accepts exile with perfect composure — the dharmic hero who renounces a kingdom without complaint.

texts.title: Ramayana: Sita's Trial by Fire
texts.description: Sita walks through fire to prove her purity after captivity — the most debated passage in Indian literature.

texts.title: Taittiriya Upanishad: The Five Sheaths
texts.description: The teaching of the five sheaths (koshas) — from food to bliss — that encase the true Self.

texts.title: Mundaka Upanishad: Two Birds on One Tree
texts.description: Two birds sit on one tree: one eats the fruit, the other watches — the self and the Self.

texts.title: Vivekachudamani: The Crest-Jewel of Discrimination
texts.description: Shankara's definitive statement of Advaita Vedanta: Brahman alone is real, the world is appearance, the self is Brahman.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita 11: The Vision of the Universal Form
texts.description: Arjuna sees Krishna's true form — all of reality, all beings, all time contained in one infinite body.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita 2.47-53: Act Without Attachment
texts.description: Krishna's core teaching: you have the right to action alone, never to its fruits.

texts.title: Rig Veda 10.14: Hymn to Yama, Lord of the Dead
texts.description: The oldest Vedic hymn on death and the afterlife: Yama, the first mortal who died, now rules the realm of the ancestors.

texts.title: Rig Veda 10.85: The Wedding Hymn
texts.description: The sacred marriage hymn still recited at Hindu weddings — Surya, daughter of the Sun, weds Soma the Moon.

texts.title: Mulamadhyamakakarika: The Root Verses of the Middle Way
texts.description: Nagarjuna's foundational text of Mahayana philosophy: nothing has inherent existence, everything arises dependently.

texts.title: Bodhicharyavatara: The Way of the Bodhisattva
texts.description: Shantideva's masterwork on compassion: the aspiration to attain enlightenment for the sake of all sentient beings.

texts.title: Buddhacarita: The Life of the Buddha
texts.description: The first full biography of the Buddha: from his miraculous birth to his enlightenment under the Bodhi tree.

texts.title: Dhammapada 14: The Awakened One
texts.description: The Buddha on what it means to be truly awake: seeing the world as it actually is.

texts.title: Metta Sutta: Discourse on Loving-Kindness
texts.description: The Buddha's teaching on universal loving-kindness — radiating goodwill to all beings in all directions.

texts.title: Lankavatara Sutra: Mind-Only
texts.description: The key text of the Mind-Only school: all phenomena are projections of consciousness, and liberation is recognizing this.

texts.title: Vimalakirti Sutra: The Thunderous Silence
texts.description: A layman bests all the monks and bodhisattvas in debate — and his final teaching is silence.

texts.title: Milinda Panha: The Questions of King Milinda
texts.description: The Greek king Menander debates the Buddhist monk Nagasena: is there a self? The chariot analogy.

texts.title: Apocryphon of John: The Secret Teaching
texts.source: Nag Hammadi Codex II; Berlin Codex
texts.description: The most important Gnostic text: how the supreme God produced emanations, how Sophia's error created the flawed material world.

texts.title: Gospel of Thomas: The Hidden Sayings
texts.description: 114 secret sayings of Jesus, many with no parallel in the canonical gospels.

texts.title: Pistis Sophia: The Repentance of Wisdom
texts.description: Sophia, trapped in the lower realms, sings thirteen repentances to the Light — a Gnostic drama of fall and redemption.

texts.title: Ghazals: The Wine of the Beloved
texts.description: Hafiz of Shiraz — the poet Iranians know by heart: love, wine, hypocrisy, and the hidden face of God.

texts.title: The Conference of the Birds
texts.description: The birds of the world journey to find the Simorgh, their king — only to discover that they themselves are the Simorgh.

texts.title: Layla and Majnun: Love unto Madness
texts.description: The Romeo and Juliet of the East: Qays' love for Layla drives him to madness, wandering the desert speaking to animals.

texts.title: Shahnameh: Rostam and Sohrab
texts.description: The most tragic episode of the Shahnameh: the hero Rostam unknowingly kills his own son Sohrab in single combat.

texts.title: Shahnameh: Zahhak the Serpent King
texts.description: The tyrant Zahhak, with serpents growing from his shoulders fed on human brains, rules for a thousand years until the blacksmith Kaveh raises his leather apron as a banner of revolution.

texts.title: Sundiata: An Epic of Old Mali
texts.description: The foundation epic of the Mali Empire: Sundiata, the crippled prince, rises to defeat the sorcerer-king and establish the greatest empire in West African history.

texts.title: Akan Creation: Nyame and the Golden Stool
texts.description: The Akan creation narrative: Nyame the sky god creates the world, and the Golden Stool descends from heaven to establish the Ashanti kingdom.

texts.title: Igbo Cosmology: Chukwu and the Four Market Days
texts.description: The Igbo creation account: Chukwu, the great spirit, orders the world into four market days and sends Chi as each person's guardian spirit.

texts.title: Dogon Cosmogony: Amma and the Cosmic Egg
texts.description: The Dogon creation narrative: Amma vibrates the cosmic egg, producing the Nommo spirits and the spiral structure of the universe.

texts.title: Kebra Nagast: The Ark Arrives in Ethiopia
texts.description: The moment the Ark of the Covenant arrives in Aksum — Ethiopia becomes the new Israel.

texts.title: Popol Vuh: The Hero Twins in Xibalba
texts.description: Hunahpu and Xbalanque descend to the underworld, outwit the lords of death, and become the sun and moon.

texts.title: Huehuetlatolli: The Ancient Words of the Elders
texts.description: Aztec moral philosophy passed from parent to child: how to live with honor, humility, and devotion to the gods.

texts.title: The Five Suns: Complete Cosmological Cycle
texts.description: The complete Aztec creation cycle: four previous worlds destroyed by jaguars, wind, fire, and flood before our current Fifth Sun.

texts.title: The Dresden Codex: The Flood and the New Creation
texts.description: The only surviving pre-Columbian Maya text on the great flood: celestial serpents pour water upon the world, destroying and renewing it.

texts.title: Flower and Song: Aztec Philosophical Poetry
texts.source: Cantares Mexicanos; Romances de los Señores
texts.description: Aztec poets ask the ultimate questions: is anything real? Do we truly live on earth? Only flower and song endure.

texts.title: The Tale of Igor's Campaign
texts.description: The greatest work of medieval Russian literature: Prince Igor's disastrous campaign against the Polovtsians, laced with pagan imagery.

texts.title: Bylina: Ilya Muromets and the Nightingale Robber
texts.description: Russia's greatest folk hero, paralyzed for 33 years, receives superhuman strength and rides to defend Kiev.

texts.title: Slavic Creation: The World Tree
texts.description: The Slavic cosmos organized around the World Tree: its crown reaches heaven, its roots descend to the underworld.

texts.title: Domostroi: How to Order Your House and Your Soul
texts.description: The Russian household manual: practical wisdom on governance, piety, hospitality, and the ordering of domestic life.

texts.title: Tao Te Ching 80: The Small State
texts.description: The Taoist utopia: a small country where people are content, never traveling to the neighboring village whose roosters they can hear.

texts.title: Han Feizi: The Five Vermin
texts.description: The coldest political philosophy ever written: virtue is useless, only laws and punishments can govern men.

texts.title: Shiji: The Grand Historian's Self-Account
texts.description: Sima Qian explains why he endured castration rather than death: to complete his father's history and make his name last forever.

texts.title: Platform Sutra: Sudden Enlightenment
texts.description: The Sixth Patriarch of Chan Buddhism teaches that enlightenment is sudden, not gradual — mind itself is Buddha.

texts.title: Drinking Alone Under the Moon
texts.description: Li Bai raises his cup to the moon and his shadow, making three companions where there was one — the most beloved poem in Chinese.

texts.title: Spring View
texts.description: Du Fu surveys the ruins of the capital during the An Lushan Rebellion: the nation is shattered, but mountains and rivers remain.

texts.title: Liezi: The Yang Zhu Chapter
texts.description: The most hedonistic text in Chinese philosophy: since we all die, why not pursue pleasure?

texts.title: Mozi: Universal Love
texts.description: Mozi's radical argument: if everyone loved others as they love themselves, war, theft, and hatred would cease.

texts.title: Book of the Dead, Spell 30B: The Heart Scarab
texts.description: The plea to one's own heart not to testify against the soul at judgment: the most commonly inscribed funerary spell.

texts.title: Dispute Between a Man and His Ba
texts.description: The oldest known text on suicide and the meaning of life: a man argues with his own soul about whether death is preferable to living.

texts.title: The Eloquent Peasant
texts.description: A peasant robbed of his goods delivers nine extraordinary speeches on justice to the magistrate — the first legal rhetoric.

texts.title: Voluspa 44-66: Ragnarok
texts.description: The end of the world: the gods march to their final battle, the sun turns black, the earth sinks — and rises again, green and renewed.

texts.title: The Lay of Sigurd: Dragon-Slayer
texts.description: Sigurd slays the dragon Fafnir, tastes its blood, and understands the speech of birds — the core myth behind the Nibelungenlied.

texts.title: Yasna 45: The Two Paths
texts.description: Zoroaster presents the fundamental moral choice: the path of truth (asha) or the path of the lie (druj).

texts.title: Yasna 51: Zoroaster's Lament
texts.description: The prophet's personal lament: surrounded by enemies, he asks Ahura Mazda when righteousness will prevail.

texts.title: Greater Bundahishn: The Full Creation Account
texts.description: The complete Zoroastrian creation narrative: Ohrmazd creates the world in six stages over 6,000 years, while Ahriman plots its destruction.

texts.title: Denkard: Encyclopedia of Zoroastrian Knowledge
texts.description: The most comprehensive surviving encyclopedia of Zoroastrian thought: theology, cosmology, ethics, and the structure of the soul.

texts.title: Bardo Thodol: The Peaceful and Wrathful Deities
texts.description: The detailed descriptions of the 42 peaceful and 58 wrathful deities encountered in the bardo — all projections of one's own mind.

texts.title: Three Principal Aspects of the Path
texts.description: Tsongkhapa distills the entire Buddhist path into three essentials: renunciation, bodhicitta, and the correct view of emptiness.

texts.title: Treasury of the Dharmadhatu
texts.description: Longchenpa's masterwork on Dzogchen: the nature of mind is primordially pure, spontaneously perfect.

texts.title: Milarepa: Song in the Snow Cave
texts.description: Milarepa sings in a cave during a blizzard, his cotton robe his only covering: inner heat conquers outer cold.

texts.title: Uttaradhyayana Sutra: The Last Teachings of Mahavira
texts.description: Mahavira's final discourse before death: on karma, non-violence, and the path to liberation.

texts.title: Samayasara: The Soul's Essence
texts.description: Kundakunda's analysis of the soul's true nature: pure, infinite, conscious, and completely distinct from matter.

texts.title: Samguk Yusa: Buddhism Arrives in Korea
texts.description: The monk Iryeon records how Buddhism transformed the Three Kingdoms of Korea and shaped Korean civilization.

texts.title: Tale of Chunhyang: Love and Justice
texts.description: Korea's greatest love story: a nobleman's son loves a courtesan's daughter; she endures torture rather than submit to a corrupt magistrate.

texts.title: The Creation: Mireuk and Seokga
texts.description: The Korean creation myth: Mireuk (Maitreya) creates the world, but the trickster Seokga steals it through a flower-growing contest.

texts.title: Ramakien: The Thai Ramayana
texts.source: Royal Thai manuscripts; Rama I version (1797)
texts.description: Thailand's national epic: the Ramayana retold through Thai Buddhist sensibility, with Hanuman as the beloved trickster-hero.

texts.title: The Glass Palace Chronicle: Origins of Burma
texts.description: The Burmese royal chronicle tracing the kings of Burma back to the lineage of the Buddha himself.

texts.title: Kalevala: The Forging of the Sampo
texts.description: The smith Ilmarinen forges the Sampo — a mysterious magical mill that grinds out prosperity — the most coveted object in Finnish mythology.

texts.title: Kalevala: Vainamoinen's Kantele
texts.description: The sage Vainamoinen builds the kantele from a pike's jawbone and plays so beautifully that all of nature weeps.

texts.title: Corpus Hermeticum I: Poimandres
texts.description: The divine mind Poimandres reveals to Hermes the creation of the world, the descent of souls, and the way of return to God.

texts.title: Corpus Hermeticum XIII: The Secret Discourse on Rebirth
texts.description: Hermes teaches his son Tat the mystery of regeneration: how to silence the twelve tormentors and be reborn in God.

texts.title: The Emerald Tablet
texts.source: Arabic original (Kitab Sirr al-Khaliqa); Latin translation
texts.description: The most famous alchemical text: 'As above, so below' — the entire Hermetic worldview in a single page.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet I: The Coming of Enkidu
texts.description: The opening of the oldest epic: Gilgamesh, tyrant and hero, and the wild man Enkidu created by the gods to be his equal.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet X: The Tavern at the Edge of the World
texts.description: Gilgamesh reaches the tavern-keeper Siduri at the edge of the world. She tells him: the life you seek you will never find.

texts.title: The Descent of Inanna to the Underworld
texts.description: The goddess Inanna descends through seven gates, stripped of her power at each one, to confront her sister Death.

texts.title: The Epic of Aqhat
texts.description: A mortal refuses immortality from a goddess and pays with his life — the Canaanite meditation on human limits.

texts.title: The Epic of Keret: The Righteous King
texts.description: King Keret loses his entire family, marches to siege a city to win a new bride, and faces a divine test of his righteousness.

texts.title: Lamp for the Path to Enlightenment
texts.description: Atisha's concise guide that organized the entire Buddhist path into three levels of practitioner.

texts.title: Abhidharmakosa: Treasury of Higher Knowledge
texts.description: The most influential systematic philosophy in Buddhist history: the classification of all phenomena.

texts.title: Madhyamakavatara: Entering the Middle Way
texts.description: Chandrakirti's masterwork joining Nagarjuna's emptiness philosophy with the bodhisattva path.

texts.title: Pramanasamuccaya: Compendium of Valid Cognition
texts.description: The foundation of Buddhist logic: there are exactly two sources of knowledge — perception and inference.

texts.title: The Bloodstream Sermon
texts.description: Bodhidharma's core teaching: the mind is the Buddha, and all phenomena are empty.

texts.title: Song of Zazen
texts.description: Hakuin's verse on the identity of samsara and nirvana: this very place is the lotus land, this very body the Buddha.

texts.title: Rissho Ankoku Ron: Establishing the Right Dharma
texts.description: Nichiren's prophetic treatise: the calamities afflicting Japan are caused by abandoning the true dharma of the Lotus Sutra.

texts.title: Samyutta Nikaya: The Fire Sermon
texts.description: The Buddha declares everything is burning — with passion, aversion, and delusion. T.S. Eliot quoted this in The Waste Land.

texts.title: Gospel of Judas: The Secret Account
texts.description: Jesus laughs at the disciples' ignorance and reveals to Judas alone the mysteries of the cosmos.

texts.title: The Exegesis on the Soul
texts.description: The soul falls from heaven into prostitution in the material world, repents, and is restored to her divine bridegroom.

texts.title: The Dialogue of the Savior
texts.description: A dialogue between Jesus and three disciples on the nature of light, darkness, and the dissolution of the body.

texts.title: Eugnostos the Blessed: The Structure of the Divine
texts.source: Nag Hammadi Codex III and V
texts.description: A philosophical treatise on the structure of the divine realm — the Unbegotten Father, the Self-Generated, and their emanations.

texts.title: The Sophia of Jesus Christ
texts.source: Nag Hammadi Codex III; Berlin Codex
texts.description: The risen Jesus teaches his disciples about the invisible God, the fall of Sophia, and the escape from the material world.

texts.title: Second Treatise of the Great Seth
texts.description: Christ reveals that he was not truly crucified — a spiritual substitute suffered on the cross while he laughed above.

texts.title: The Concept of Our Great Power
texts.description: A Gnostic apocalypse: the ages of the flesh, the psyche, and the spirit, ending with the dissolution of the material world.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita 3.19-26: Action Without Attachment
texts.description: Krishna teaches that even the wise must act — but without clinging to results.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita 6.5-7: The Self Is the Friend of the Self
texts.description: The self must be raised by the self; the self is the friend of the self, and the self is the enemy of the self.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita 9.26-29: A Leaf, a Flower, a Fruit
texts.description: Krishna's simplest teaching on devotion: offer me even a leaf, a flower, a fruit, or water — with love, I will accept it.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita 12.13-20: The Beloved Devotee
texts.description: Krishna describes who is dearest to him: free from hatred, compassionate, without ego, equal in pleasure and pain.

texts.title: Mahabharata: The Questions of the Yaksha
texts.description: A water spirit tests Yudhishthira with riddles on the nature of dharma — the most philosophical passage in the Mahabharata.

texts.title: Ramayana: Hanuman's Leap Across the Ocean
texts.description: Hanuman expands to gigantic size and leaps across the ocean to Lanka — the greatest feat of devotion in Indian literature.

texts.title: Prashna Upanishad: Six Questions on the Nature of Reality
texts.description: Six seekers each ask one question about the nature of reality — on creation, the vital breath, the mind, sleep, meditation, and the supreme person.

texts.title: Svetasvatara Upanishad: The One God Within
texts.description: The earliest Upanishad to identify Brahman with a personal God — Rudra-Shiva — who dwells within all beings.

texts.title: Aitareya Upanishad: Consciousness Is Brahman
texts.description: The teaching that consciousness (prajnanam) is Brahman — one of the four mahavakyas (great sayings) of the Upanishads.

texts.title: Ramcharitmanas: The Lake of Rama's Deeds
texts.description: Tulsidas retells the Ramayana in Hindi — the most widely read devotional text in northern India.

texts.title: The Dohas of Kabir: The Guest Is Inside
texts.description: Kabir's couplets demolish religious hypocrisy: God is not in the temple or the mosque but within you.

texts.title: The Poems of Mirabai: I Have Found My Lord
texts.description: Mirabai's ecstatic devotional poems to Krishna — the princess who left her palace to sing in the streets.

texts.title: Yoga Sutras 2.1-11: The Five Afflictions
texts.description: Patanjali identifies the five root causes of suffering: ignorance, ego, attachment, aversion, and clinging to life.

texts.title: Yoga Sutras 3.1-8: Dharana, Dhyana, Samadhi
texts.description: The three inner limbs of yoga: concentration, meditation, and absorption — together called samyama.

texts.title: The Destruction of Da Derga's Hostel
texts.description: King Conaire Mor, bound by magical prohibitions he cannot help but break, meets his doom at a besieged hostel.

texts.title: The Death of Cuchulainn
texts.description: The greatest Irish hero, bound by magical obligations, ties himself to a standing stone so he can die on his feet.

texts.title: Buile Suibhne: The Madness of Sweeney
texts.description: A king cursed to live as a bird-man in the trees, composing the most beautiful nature poetry in medieval Irish literature.

texts.title: The Voyage of Maelduin
texts.description: Maelduin sails through 31 fantastic islands — the text that inspired the Voyage of St. Brendan and the Odyssey comparisons.

texts.title: The Adventure of Connla
texts.description: A fairy woman calls Connla to the Otherworld. He sails away in a crystal boat and is never seen again.

texts.title: Mountain Poems
texts.description: The warrior-monk Saigyo's poems of mountain solitude — cherry blossoms, moonlight, and the loneliness that leads to awakening.

texts.title: Crazy Cloud Anthology
texts.description: Ikkyu, the scandalous Zen master who frequented brothels and wrote poems about enlightenment in bed.

texts.title: One Robe, One Bowl: Poems of Ryokan
texts.description: The gentlest poet in Japanese literature: a Zen monk who played with children and let thieves take everything.

texts.title: Takasago: A Noh Play on Eternal Love
texts.description: Two pine trees, separated by a bay, are revealed as an ancient married couple — love transcending form.

texts.title: Uji Shui Monogatari: Tales from Uji
texts.description: 197 tales of monks, warriors, thieves, and tricksters — the great medieval Japanese story collection.

texts.title: Book of the Dead, Spell 64: Coming Forth by Day
texts.description: The master spell for emerging from the underworld into the light of day — the deceased becomes like the sun.

texts.title: Hymn to the Nile
texts.description: A hymn to Hapi, the Nile god, whose annual flood brings life to Egypt — one of the oldest nature poems.

texts.title: Book of the Dead, Spell 175: Dialogue with Atum
texts.description: A dialogue with the creator god about the end of the world: eventually everything will return to the primordial waters.

texts.title: Thrymskvidha: Thor in a Wedding Dress
texts.description: Thor's hammer is stolen by a giant who demands Freyja as bride. Thor dresses as Freyja and retrieves it.

texts.title: Ghazals: The Beloved's Face
texts.description: Hafiz on the face of the Beloved — human or divine, wine or worship, the ambiguity is the point.

texts.title: Ghazals: Last Night the Angel
texts.description: Hafiz on the angel of knowledge bringing him the cup of divine love — the most famous ghazal.

texts.title: Tadhkirat al-Awliya: Memorial of the Saints
texts.description: Attar's biographies of 72 Sufi saints — their miracles, their madness, their closeness to God.

texts.title: Instructions for Practical Living
texts.description: Wang Yangming's revolutionary teaching: knowledge and action are one; the innate moral knowledge (liangzhi) of the heart-mind is sufficient.

texts.title: Reflections on Things at Hand
texts.description: Zhu Xi's anthology of Neo-Confucian philosophy: the investigation of things leads to the extension of knowledge.

texts.title: Quiet Night Thought
texts.description: The most memorized poem in the Chinese language: moonlight on the floor, homesickness for home.

texts.title: Moonlit Night
texts.description: Du Fu in prison thinks of his wife watching the same moon from their home in Fuzhou.

texts.title: Deer Park
texts.description: The quintessential Chan Buddhist poem: an empty mountain, a human voice, a shaft of light on green moss.

texts.title: Cold Mountain Poems
texts.description: Poems scratched on rocks and trees by a laughing hermit — the original counterculture literature.

texts.title: The Song of Everlasting Sorrow
texts.description: The love of Emperor Xuanzong and Yang Guifei — destroyed by rebellion, mourned for eternity.

texts.title: Rhapsody on the Red Cliff
texts.description: Su Shi's meditation on impermanence while boating at night on the Yangtze — one of the greatest prose poems in Chinese.

texts.title: Tonalamatl: The Book of Days
texts.description: The Aztec sacred calendar: 260 days, each governed by a deity, determining character, destiny, and fortune.

texts.title: The Poems of Nezahualcoyotl: The Poet-King
texts.description: The warrior-philosopher-king of Texcoco composed poems questioning the meaning of existence with unsettling clarity.

texts.title: Popol Vuh: The Destruction of the Wooden Men
texts.description: The gods' second creation — humans made from wood — are destroyed because they have no hearts and cannot worship.

texts.title: Popol Vuh: The Blood Maiden and the Lords of Death
texts.description: The skull of Hun Hunahpu spits into the hand of the maiden Xquic, conceiving the Hero Twins.

texts.title: Sundiata: The Sorcerer's Defeat
texts.description: Sundiata confronts Soumaoro the sorcerer-king, who is invulnerable to weapons — and defeats him with a rooster's spur tipped with the spirit of his ancestors.

texts.title: Anansi: How the Spider Got All Stories
texts.description: Anansi the spider tricks the sky god Nyame into giving him ownership of all the world's stories.

texts.title: The Bantu Creation: How Death Came to the World
texts.description: God sends the chameleon with a message of eternal life, but the lizard outruns it with a message of death.

texts.title: The Letter of Prester John
texts.description: The legendary letter from an all-powerful Christian king in the East — sparking centuries of European exploration.

texts.title: Bylina: Sadko and the Sea King
texts.description: A merchant-musician is taken to the bottom of the sea and must play his gusli for the Sea King — his music causes storms.

texts.title: Bylina: Volga the Sorcerer-Prince
texts.description: Prince Volga, born of a serpent, commands the languages of all animals and shapeshifts to win battles.

texts.title: Slavic Mythology: Morana and the Cycle of Death
texts.description: The Slavic goddess of death and winter, burned in effigy each spring to ensure the return of life.

texts.title: Bardo Thodol: The Clear Light of Death
texts.description: At the moment of death, the clear light of reality dawns — if recognized, liberation is immediate.

texts.title: Ornament of the Middle Way
texts.description: Shantarakshita's synthesis of Madhyamaka emptiness and Yogacara mind-only — the philosophical foundation of Tibetan Buddhism.

texts.title: Vendidad: The Law Against Demons
texts.description: The Zoroastrian law code on purity, pollution, and the battle against evil — the most detailed ritual text in the Avesta.

texts.title: The Book of Arda Viraf: Journey Through Heaven and Hell
texts.description: A Zoroastrian priest drinks a sacred potion and travels through the seven levels of heaven and the punishments of hell.

texts.title: Dasavaikalika Sutra: Ten Chapters on Religious Life
texts.description: The Jain monastic manual: rules for monks on non-violence, speech, food, and the conquest of the passions.

texts.title: Niyamasara: The Essence of Restraint
texts.description: Kundakunda on the path to liberation: right knowledge, right faith, right conduct, right austerity.

texts.title: Hyangga: The Oldest Korean Poems
texts.description: The 25 surviving hyangga — Korea's oldest native poetry, composed in the Silla period.

texts.title: Muga: Korean Shamanist Ritual Songs
texts.description: The ritual songs of Korean shamans (mudang) who mediate between the living and the dead.

texts.title: Phra Aphai Mani: The Prince's Flute
texts.description: The prince whose magical flute-playing enchants even sea ogresses — Thailand's most beloved literary hero.

texts.title: Arjunawiwaha: Arjuna's Wedding
texts.description: The Javanese masterpiece: Arjuna meditates so deeply that the gods send celestial nymphs to tempt him.

texts.title: Kalevala: Louhi, Mistress of the North
texts.description: The sorceress Louhi steals the sun and moon, plunging the world into darkness until Vainamoinen tricks her.

texts.title: Kalevala: The Tragedy of Kullervo
texts.description: The slave Kullervo, abused since birth, unknowingly commits incest and kills himself — the darkest story in Finnish mythology. Tolkien based Turin Turambar on this.

texts.title: Corpus Hermeticum IV: The Mixing Bowl
texts.description: God sends a great mixing bowl of Nous (Mind) to earth. Those who bathe in it receive knowledge; those who don't remain ignorant.

texts.title: Corpus Hermeticum VII: Ignorance of God Is the Greatest Evil
texts.description: A short, fierce discourse on the supreme evil: not knowing God.

texts.title: Corpus Hermeticum XI: Mind to Hermes
texts.description: Mind itself speaks to Hermes about the nature of God, the cosmos, and the speed of thought.

texts.title: Nergal and Ereshkigal: Love in the Underworld
texts.description: The sky god Nergal descends to the underworld, seduces Queen Ereshkigal, and becomes king of the dead.

texts.title: The Myth of Etana: Flight to Heaven
texts.description: King Etana rides an eagle to heaven seeking the plant of birth — the oldest story of human flight.

texts.title: The Erra Epic: God of Plague and War
texts.description: The god Erra, bored and restless, deceives Marduk and unleashes devastating war and plague upon Babylon.

texts.title: The Birth of the Gracious Gods
texts.description: The birth of Dawn (Shahar) and Dusk (Shalim) from the union of El with two women — a text linking Canaanite and Hebrew myth.

texts.title: The Wedding of Nikkal and Yarikh
texts.description: The moon god Yarikh pays the bride-price for the moon goddess Nikkal — the oldest known wedding hymn.

texts.title: Enneads I.6: On Beauty
texts.description: Plotinus on how the soul recognizes beauty because it is itself beautiful — and how to ascend from physical to spiritual beauty.

texts.title: Enneads III.8: On Nature, Contemplation, and the One
texts.description: Plotinus argues that all of nature — even stones and plants — are engaged in a form of contemplation.

texts.title: Enneads V.1: The Three Primal Hypostases
texts.description: Plotinus on the One, Intellect, and Soul — the three levels of divine reality from which all things emanate.

texts.title: Enneads V.5: That the Intelligibles Are Not Outside Intellect
texts.description: Plotinus argues the Forms are not external objects but the very thinking of divine Intellect.

texts.title: Enneads VI.7: How the Multitude of the Forms Came Into Being
texts.description: Plotinus on how the infinite variety of the world flows from the One's superabundant goodness.

texts.title: Elements of Theology: The One and the Many
texts.description: Proclus systematizes Neoplatonism into 211 propositions: from the One, through henads and intellects, to the material world.

texts.title: On the Mysteries: Theurgy and the Gods
texts.description: Iamblichus defends ritual practice (theurgy) as superior to philosophy for achieving union with the divine.

texts.title: On Abstinence from Animal Food
texts.description: Porphyry's argument for vegetarianism on philosophical and spiritual grounds — perhaps the most influential such text in antiquity.

texts.title: On the Cave of the Nymphs
texts.description: Porphyry interprets the cave in Odyssey 13 as an allegory for the soul's descent into matter and return to the divine.

texts.title: Problems and Solutions: The Ineffable
texts.description: The last head of the Platonic Academy argues that the first principle is so beyond being that even calling it 'One' says too much.

texts.title: On Isis and Osiris
texts.description: Plutarch's philosophical interpretation of the Egyptian myth — the most detailed account of Osiris in any Greek source.

texts.title: On the Daimonion of Socrates
texts.description: Plutarch explores the nature of Socrates' inner divine voice — what was it? A god? A daemon? Pure intuition?

texts.title: On the Obsolescence of Oracles
texts.description: Why have the oracles fallen silent? Plutarch's characters debate: because the daemons have departed, or because the world is aging.

texts.title: Antidosis: The Defense of Philosophy
texts.description: Isocrates defends the study of rhetoric and philosophy against those who call it useless.

texts.title: First Philippic: Against the Tyrant
texts.description: The speech that gave English the word 'philippic' — Demosthenes warns Athens that Philip of Macedon will destroy their freedom.

texts.title: Lives of the Philosophers: The Seven Sages
texts.description: The biographies and sayings of the earliest Greek philosophers — from Thales' 'Know thyself' to Solon's 'Nothing in excess.'

texts.title: Aesop's Fables: The Fox and the Grapes
texts.description: The fables that taught the ancient world to think in metaphors — and gave English half its proverbs.

texts.title: The Clouds: Against the Sophists
texts.description: Aristophanes mocks Socrates as a charlatan who teaches young men to make the worse argument the better.

texts.title: The Birds: Building a City in the Sky
texts.description: Two Athenians escape the city and persuade the birds to build Cloudcuckooland between heaven and earth — blocking the gods' sacrificial smoke.

texts.title: The Frogs: Who Is the Greatest Poet?
texts.description: Dionysus descends to Hades to bring back a great tragedian. Aeschylus and Euripides compete — Aeschylus wins.

texts.title: Hippolytus: The Destructive Power of Eros
texts.description: Phaedra's forbidden love for her stepson Hippolytus, engineered by Aphrodite, destroys them both.

texts.title: The Trojan Women: The Cost of War
texts.description: The women of Troy, their city destroyed, are divided among the Greek conquerors. The most devastating anti-war play ever written.

texts.title: Dyskolos: The Grouch
texts.description: The only complete surviving play of New Comedy: a misanthropic farmer, a lovesick young man, and the comedy of social class.

texts.title: Aeneid I: Arms and the Man
texts.description: The opening of Rome's national epic: Aeneas, driven by fate, sails from Troy to found the Roman race.

texts.title: Aeneid VI: The Descent to the Underworld
texts.description: Aeneas descends to the underworld and sees the future of Rome — the most influential katabasis after Homer.

texts.title: Eclogue 4: The Golden Age Returns
texts.description: The 'Messianic Eclogue' predicting the birth of a child who will usher in a new Golden Age — Christians read it as a prophecy of Christ.

texts.title: Metamorphoses I: In the Beginning
texts.description: Ovid's creation of the world from Chaos: the poem that transmitted Greek mythology to the entire Western tradition.

texts.title: Metamorphoses XV: The Speech of Pythagoras
texts.description: Pythagoras expounds the doctrine of metempsychosis: nothing dies, everything changes form.

texts.title: Germania: The Germans
texts.description: Tacitus' ethnographic account of the Germanic tribes — idealized as noble savages to shame decadent Rome.

texts.title: Annals XV: Nero and the Great Fire
texts.description: Tacitus records the Great Fire of Rome and Nero's persecution of Christians — the earliest detailed pagan account.

texts.title: Satires III: Against the City of Rome
texts.description: Juvenal's friend Umbricius flees Rome: the city is too corrupt, too crowded, too Greek, too dangerous to live in.

texts.title: Satires X: The Vanity of Human Wishes
texts.description: What should we pray for? Not wealth, power, beauty, or long life — all lead to ruin. Pray only for a sound mind in a sound body.

texts.title: Homilies on the Statues: The Dignity of Man
texts.description: Chrysostom preaches to a terrified Antioch after rioters destroy the emperor's statues: true dignity comes not from statues but from the image of God in each person.

texts.title: Homilies on Lazarus: Wealth and Poverty
texts.description: Chrysostom's devastating attack on the rich who ignore the poor lying at their gates.

texts.title: On the Priesthood III: The Terror of the Office
texts.description: Why Chrysostom fled ordination: the priesthood demands a holiness beyond human capacity.

texts.title: Hexaemeron: On the Six Days of Creation
texts.description: Basil's nine homilies on Genesis 1: how to read creation scientifically and spiritually at once.

texts.title: On the Holy Spirit: Co-Equal with the Father
texts.description: Basil's defense of the divinity of the Holy Spirit — the text that settled Trinitarian theology.

texts.title: Third Theological Oration: On God's Nature
texts.description: Gregory argues that God's nature is ultimately incomprehensible — we can know that God is, not what God is.

texts.title: Fifth Theological Oration: On the Holy Spirit
texts.description: Gregory completes the doctrine of the Trinity: the Spirit proceeds from the Father, not as generated but as 'breathed forth.'

texts.title: On the Duties of the Clergy
texts.description: The first systematic Christian ethics, modeled on Cicero: how the virtues of prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance apply to clergy.

texts.title: Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith
texts.description: The last great systematic theology of the patristic era: a summa of everything the Church Fathers taught about God, creation, Christ, and the last things.

texts.title: Four Hundred Chapters on Love
texts.description: Maximus' spiritual aphorisms: love is the highest form of knowledge, and perfect love casts out all self-interest.

texts.title: The Mystical Theology: Darkness Beyond Light
texts.description: The most influential text on apophatic theology: God is known by unknowing, reached by leaving behind all concepts.

texts.title: On the Divine Names
texts.description: How can we name the unnameable? Dionysius explores every name of God — Good, Light, Beauty, Love, Being — and shows how each fails.

texts.title: Conferences: On the Goal of the Monk
texts.description: The Egyptian monk Abba Moses teaches: the immediate goal is purity of heart; the ultimate goal is the kingdom of heaven.

texts.title: Consolation of Philosophy: Fortune's Wheel
texts.description: Boethius, sentenced to death, is visited by Lady Philosophy. She teaches him that Fortune's gifts were never truly his.

texts.title: Summa Theologiae I.2: The Five Ways
texts.description: Thomas Aquinas' five proofs for God's existence: motion, causation, contingency, degrees of perfection, and design.

texts.title: Summa Theologiae I-II.3: The Beatific Vision
texts.description: Aquinas on the ultimate happiness: seeing God face to face. Not faith, not works, not contemplation — but direct vision.

texts.title: German Sermon 1: The Detached Soul
texts.description: Eckhart's radical mysticism: the soul must become so empty that even God — as concept — disappears. Then the true God enters.

texts.title: German Sermon 52: Blessed Are the Poor in Spirit
texts.description: The most radical sermon ever preached: true poverty means wanting nothing, knowing nothing, having nothing — not even God.

texts.title: Revelations of Divine Love: All Shall Be Well
texts.description: The first book written in English by a woman: Julian's visions of Christ's passion and the assurance that all shall be well.

texts.title: Interior Castle: The Seventh Mansion
texts.description: Teresa's map of the soul: seven mansions leading to the center where God dwells. The seventh is spiritual marriage.

texts.title: Dark Night of the Soul
texts.description: John of the Cross on the soul's purification through darkness: God strips away all consolation so the soul can receive pure light.

texts.title: On Loving God
texts.description: Bernard's four degrees of love: loving self for self's sake, loving God for self's sake, loving God for God's sake, loving self for God's sake.

texts.title: The Imitation of Christ I: Counsels Useful for Spiritual Life
texts.description: The most widely read devotional book after the Bible: vanity of vanities, all is vanity, except to love God and serve him alone.

texts.title: Kitab al-Tawasin: The Book of the Letter Ta-Sin
texts.description: Al-Hallaj's mystical treatise on the light of Muhammad, the fall of Iblis (Satan), and the nature of divine love.

texts.title: Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya: The Meccan Revelations
texts.description: The most comprehensive work of Sufi metaphysics: 560 chapters on every aspect of the relationship between God and creation.

texts.title: Al-Munqidh min al-Dalal: Deliverance from Error
texts.description: Al-Ghazali's intellectual autobiography: how he lost faith through philosophy, regained it through Sufism.

texts.title: Divan-i Shams: Out Beyond Ideas
texts.description: The most famous lyric in Sufi literature: beyond rightdoing and wrongdoing, there is a field.

texts.title: Al-Madina al-Fadila: The Virtuous City
texts.description: Al-Farabi's political philosophy: the ideal city modeled on Plato's Republic, ruled by a philosopher-prophet.

texts.title: The Flying Man: The Soul Knows Itself
texts.description: Avicenna's thought experiment: if a man were created floating in the void, he would still know 'I am.'

texts.title: Al-Isharat: Pointers and Reminders
texts.description: Avicenna's mature philosophy: the distinction between essence and existence, the necessary being, and the emanation of the world.

texts.title: Fasl al-Maqal: The Decisive Treatise
texts.description: Averroes argues that Islamic law not only permits but requires the study of philosophy.

texts.title: On First Philosophy
texts.description: The first systematic work of philosophy written in Arabic: the nature of truth and the existence of a first cause.

texts.title: Analects 9.5: Heaven's Culture Cannot Be Destroyed
texts.description: When threatened, Confucius declares that if Heaven intended this culture to survive, no one can destroy it.

texts.title: Analects 12.22: Raise the Straight and Place Them Over the Crooked
texts.description: The Confucian principle of governance: promote honest people and the dishonest will be reformed.

texts.title: Analects 17.2: By Nature Near, By Practice Far
texts.description: Human nature is similar at birth; it is practice and habit that make people different.

texts.title: Mencius 7A.4: All Things Are Complete Within Me
texts.description: Mencius declares that everything needed for moral perfection is already within the human heart.

texts.title: Mencius 1A.7: The Ox Mountain Parable
texts.description: A mountain stripped bare still has shoots of goodness — like human nature, degraded by circumstance but never destroyed.

texts.title: Chunqiu Fanlu: Luxuriant Dew of the Spring and Autumn
texts.description: Dong Zhongshu's synthesis of Confucianism with cosmology: Heaven, Earth, and Humanity form a triad.

texts.title: Commentary on the Yijing: Principle and Material Force
texts.description: Cheng Yi distinguishes principle (li) from material force (qi) — the foundation of Neo-Confucian metaphysics.

texts.title: Tao Te Ching 2: The Relativity of Opposites
texts.description: When the world knows beauty as beauty, ugliness arises. When it knows good as good, evil appears.

texts.title: Tao Te Ching 16: Returning to the Root
texts.description: Attain the utmost emptiness. Hold firm to stillness. All things flourish and return to the root.

texts.title: Tao Te Ching 33: Knowing Others and Knowing Yourself
texts.description: Knowing others is intelligence; knowing yourself is true wisdom.

texts.title: Tao Te Ching 64: The Journey of a Thousand Miles
texts.description: The most quoted Taoist proverb: a journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.

texts.title: Zhuangzi: Cook Ting Carves an Ox
texts.description: Cook Ting moves his knife through the spaces in the joints, never touching bone — the art of following the natural grain.

texts.title: Zhuangzi: The Useless Tree
texts.description: A gnarled tree survives because it is useless — the Taoist paradox of the value of uselessness.

texts.title: Baopuzi: The Master Who Embraces Simplicity
texts.description: Ge Hong's defense of alchemy, longevity techniques, and the Taoist immortals — the most systematic early text on Chinese alchemy.

texts.title: Silla Buddhist Poetry: The Way Home
texts.description: Buddhist monks of the Silla kingdom composed poems on impermanence and the path to awakening.

texts.title: Samguk Yusa: Wonhyo's Enlightenment
texts.description: The monk Wonhyo drinks from a skull in the night, is enlightened in the morning: all reality is mind.

texts.title: Kebra Nagast: The Queen of Sheba Visits Solomon
texts.description: The full account of Makeda (Sheba) testing Solomon's wisdom — the foundation of Ethiopian royal theology.

texts.title: Kebra Nagast: Menelik and the Covenant
texts.description: Menelik, son of Solomon and Sheba, brings the Ark of the Covenant to Ethiopia — the transfer of divine election.

texts.title: Hatata: An Ethiopian Rationalist Philosophy
texts.description: An Ethiopian philosopher independently develops rational theology: rejecting all religions as human inventions while affirming God through reason alone.

texts.title: Kalevala: The Death and Resurrection of Lemminkainen
texts.description: The reckless hero Lemminkainen is killed and dismembered; his mother gathers the pieces and sings him back to life.

texts.title: Kalevala: Vainamoinen's Final Departure
texts.description: The old sage sails away in a copper boat, leaving behind his songs — like Arthur departing for Avalon.

texts.title: Khmer Creation: Preah Thong and Neang Neak
texts.description: The Cambodian foundation myth: an Indian prince marries a naga princess, and from their union the Khmer civilization is born.

texts.title: Tantu Panggelaran: How Java Was Made Flat
texts.description: The gods move Mount Meru from India to Java, flattening the island in the process — the Javanese creation myth.

texts.title: Anekantavada: The Doctrine of Many-Sidedness
texts.description: The Jain epistemological principle: reality has infinite aspects, and no single perspective captures the whole truth.

texts.title: Mahavira's Last Sermon: The Final Teaching
texts.description: Mahavira's final words before achieving moksha: complete non-attachment, complete non-violence, complete liberation.

texts.title: The Council of El: The Father of Gods
texts.description: El, the father of the gods, presides over the divine assembly at the source of the two rivers — the Canaanite prototype of Psalm 82.

texts.title: The Rephaim Texts: The Shades of the Dead
texts.description: The Rephaim — ancient warrior-kings now dwelling as shades — are summoned from the underworld to a feast.

texts.title: Bylina: Dobrynya and the Dragon
texts.description: The bogatyr Dobrynya fights the dragon Zmey Gorynych to save a captive princess.

texts.title: Perun and Veles: The Cosmic Struggle
texts.description: The thunder god Perun battles the serpent-god Veles — the core myth of Slavic religion, paralleling Thor vs. Jormungandr.

texts.title: Epic of King Gesar: The Warrior from Heaven
texts.description: The longest epic in the world (over 1 million verses in some versions): the divine hero Gesar descends to earth to defeat demons.

texts.title: Milarepa: Song on the Nature of Mind
texts.description: Milarepa sings to a disciple about the nature of mind: clear, luminous, and free from the beginning.

texts.title: Yasna 46: Zoroaster's Isolation
texts.description: Zoroaster's lament of isolation: rejected by his clan, he asks Ahura Mazda where to turn for help.

texts.title: Yasna 53: The Wedding Hymn of Zoroaster's Daughter
texts.description: The only personal detail from Zoroaster's life: instructions for his daughter's wedding.

texts.title: Yoruba Proverbs: The Wisdom of the Elders
texts.description: Yoruba proverbial wisdom covering justice, patience, community, and the relationship between the visible and invisible worlds.

texts.title: Kintu: The First Man of Buganda
texts.description: The foundation myth of the Buganda kingdom: Kintu descends from heaven with one cow and must pass tests to win Nambi, daughter of the sky god.

texts.title: Mwari and the First People
texts.description: The Shona creation narrative: Mwari, the high god, creates the first people and speaks to them through the oracle of Matonjeni.

texts.title: Maya Prophecy: The Katun Wheel
texts.description: Maya cyclical prophecy: each 20-year katun brings specific events, and history repeats when the same katun returns.

texts.title: The Legend of the Four Hundred Rabbits
texts.description: The 400 rabbit gods of drunkenness — the Aztec myth explaining the many forms of intoxication.

texts.title: Asclepius: The Perfect Discourse
texts.source: Augustine, De Civitate Dei; Latin manuscripts
texts.description: Hermes prophesies the decline of Egyptian religion and the death of the gods — a passage that haunted Augustine.

texts.title: Corpus Hermeticum III: The Sacred Discourse
texts.description: A hymn of cosmic creation: God's glory fills the universe, and the human being is the culmination of creation.

texts.title: Kimiya-yi Sa'adat: The Alchemy of Happiness
texts.description: Al-Ghazali's Persian summary of the Ihya: how to transform the base metal of the nafs (ego) into the gold of the spirit.

texts.title: Tarikh al-Hind: A Study of India
texts.description: Al-Biruni's extraordinary work of comparative religion: a Muslim scholar presents Hindu philosophy with complete fairness.

texts.title: A Hymn to Inanna: Queen of Heaven
texts.description: Enheduanna's hymn to Inanna — the first named author in human history praises the most powerful goddess.

texts.title: Gilgamesh, Enkidu, and the Netherworld
texts.description: Enkidu's ghost returns from the underworld to describe the fates of the dead: a bleak Sumerian afterlife.

texts.title: Alvissmal: The Wisdom of the Dwarf
texts.description: Thor keeps a dwarf talking until dawn — when sunlight turns him to stone. A mythological dictionary of poetic language.

texts.title: Great Hymn to Osiris
texts.description: The most complete Egyptian hymn to Osiris: his murder by Set, his resurrection by Isis, and his reign over the dead.

texts.title: The Voyage of St. Brendan
texts.description: A Christian monk sails the Atlantic and finds paradise — or did Irish monks reach America before Columbus?

texts.title: The Triads of Ireland
texts.description: Over 250 proverbs organized in threes — the Irish method of condensing wisdom.

texts.description: Homily on Matthew 1 — The Genealogy of Christ

texts.title: Revelations of Divine Love Ch. 13: God Showed Me a Little Thing

texts.title: Revelations of Divine Love Ch. 27: All Shall Be Well
