texts.title: The Spiritual Canticle: Where Have You Hidden?

texts.title: Ordo Virtutum: The Play of the Virtues

texts.title: Sermons on the Song of Songs

texts.title: The Imitation of Christ II: The Inner Life
texts.description: The Imitation of Christ II — The Inner Life

texts.title: The Imitation of Christ III: On Inward Consolation
texts.description: The Imitation of Christ III — On Inward Consolation

texts.title: Enneads I.1: What Is the Living Being?
texts.description: What constitutes a living being? Not the body alone, nor the soul alone, but their union — yet the true self is the soul.

texts.title: Enneads I.2: On the Virtues
texts.description: The civic virtues are good; the purificatory virtues are better; but the highest virtues are those of the Intellect.

texts.title: Enneads I.3: On Dialectic
texts.description: Three types of people can ascend: the musician, the lover, and the philosopher. Each begins from beauty.

texts.title: Enneads II.9: Against the Gnostics
texts.description: Plotinus attacks the Gnostics: they insult the cosmos by calling it evil, when it is the best possible image of the divine.

texts.title: Enneads III.2: On Providence I
texts.description: Evil is not a positive force but a deficiency of good — like darkness, which is merely the absence of light.

texts.title: Enneads IV.3: On the Soul I
texts.description: The soul is not in the body as in a container. Rather, the body is in the soul. The soul contains the body.

texts.title: Enneads IV.8: On the Descent of the Soul
texts.description: Why did the soul descend into the body? Not by compulsion but by a kind of voluntary gravity — to bring light to the lower realms.

texts.title: Enneads V.2: On the Origin of Things Below the First
texts.description: The One produces by its very perfection — as the sun radiates light without diminishing, so the One emanates being.

texts.title: Enneads V.3: On the Knowing Hypostases
texts.description: There are three degrees of knowing: sensation, reason, and intellection. Only the last truly knows.

texts.title: Enneads VI.4: On the Omnipresence of True Being
texts.description: True being is everywhere whole. The One is nowhere and everywhere. Space cannot contain what is beyond space.

texts.title: Enneads VI.8: On Free Will and the Will of the One
texts.description: Is the One free? It is beyond freedom — it is what it wills, and wills what it is. There is no gap between its being and its willing.

texts.title: Elements of Theology: On Causation and Return
texts.description: Everything that proceeds from a cause returns to its cause. This is the law of cosmic circulation.

texts.title: Commentary on the Parmenides: The One Beyond Being
texts.description: Proclus' magnum opus: the most detailed analysis of the One in the entire Platonic tradition.

texts.title: Protrepticus: Exhortation to Philosophy
texts.description: Iamblichus exhorts students to philosophy: the soul, imprisoned in the body, can be freed only through the pursuit of wisdom.

texts.title: On the Pythagorean Life
texts.description: The biography of Pythagoras as a divine man: his miracles, his teachings, and the way of life he established.

texts.title: Commentary on Aristotle's Categories
texts.description: The last great philosophical commentary of antiquity: Simplicius synthesizes Aristotle with Plato.

texts.title: Isagoge: Introduction to Aristotle's Categories
texts.description: The textbook that taught logic to the entire medieval world: genus, species, differentia, property, accident.

texts.title: On the God of Socrates
texts.description: Apuleius explains the Platonic doctrine of daemons — intermediary beings between gods and men, including Socrates' famous sign.

texts.title: Commentary on the Timaeus: The World Soul
texts.description: The commentary that kept Plato alive in the Latin West through the Dark Ages.

texts.title: Symposium: Diotima's Ladder of Love
texts.description: Diotima teaches Socrates the ascent of love: from one beautiful body to all bodies, to the beauty of souls, to Beauty itself.

texts.title: Republic VI: The Allegory of the Sun
texts.description: As the sun illuminates the visible world, so the Form of the Good illuminates the intelligible world.

texts.title: Republic VI: The Divided Line
texts.description: Plato divides reality into four levels: images, physical objects, mathematical objects, and the Forms.

texts.title: Meno: Learning Is Recollection
texts.description: Socrates proves that learning is recollection by leading an uneducated slave boy to discover geometry.

texts.title: Phaedo: The Death of Socrates
texts.description: Socrates drinks the hemlock and dies discussing the immortality of the soul — the most famous death scene in Western literature.

texts.title: Apology: Socrates' Defense
texts.description: Socrates defends himself at trial: the unexamined life is not worth living.

texts.title: Crito: Socrates Refuses to Escape
texts.description: Socrates refuses to escape prison: one must never do wrong, even in response to wrong done to oneself.

texts.title: Gorgias: The Myth of Judgment After Death
texts.description: Socrates tells of how the dead are judged naked — stripped of wealth, reputation, and power.

texts.title: Laws X: The Existence of the Gods
texts.description: Plato's last word on theology: the soul is prior to the body, and the orderly motion of the heavens proves that gods exist.

texts.title: Rhetoric I.1: The Art of Persuasion
texts.description: Rhetoric is the counterpart of dialectic. It is the faculty of observing the available means of persuasion in any given case.

texts.title: Georgics I: Labor and the Land
texts.description: Virgil's poem on farming as a metaphor for civilization: Jupiter deliberately made agriculture difficult so humans would develop skill.

texts.title: Metamorphoses VI: Arachne's Challenge
texts.description: A mortal weaver challenges Athena and is transformed into a spider — the price of surpassing the gods.

texts.title: Metamorphoses X: Orpheus and Eurydice
texts.description: Orpheus descends to the underworld to retrieve his wife, and loses her by looking back — the most retold myth in Western art.

texts.title: Odes III.30: Exegi Monumentum
texts.description: I have built a monument more lasting than bronze. Not all of me will die.

texts.title: Satires I.9: The Bore
texts.description: Horace tries to escape an insufferable social climber on the streets of Rome — the funniest poem in Latin.

texts.title: On the Shortness of Life
texts.description: Life is not short — we waste it. Seneca's attack on busyness and his defense of the contemplative life.

texts.title: Moral Letters 1: On Saving Time
texts.description: Hold every hour in your grasp. What is yours now will belong to someone else tomorrow.

texts.title: On the Tranquility of the Mind
texts.description: How to achieve peace of mind: withdraw from crowds, limit possessions, prepare for adversity.

texts.title: Meditations IV: The View from Above
texts.description: Look at the whole earth from above and see how small all human concerns are.

texts.title: Meditations VII: The Discipline of Assent
texts.description: Say to every impression: you are an appearance and not at all the thing you appear to be.

texts.title: Enchiridion 8: Desire Nothing That Is Not In Your Power
texts.description: Do not seek to have events happen as you want them to. Wish them to happen as they do happen, and you will go on well.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 1: Arjuna's Despair
texts.description: Chapter 1 of the Bhagavad Gita: Arjuna surveys the battlefield and is overcome with grief at the prospect of killing his own kinsmen.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 2: The Yoga of Knowledge
texts.description: Chapter 2 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna teaches Arjuna the immortality of the soul and the discipline of selfless action.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 3: The Yoga of Action
texts.description: Chapter 3 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna explains karma yoga: perform your duty without attachment to results.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 4: The Yoga of Wisdom
texts.description: Chapter 4 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna reveals that he incarnates whenever dharma declines, and teaches the path of knowledge.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 5: The Yoga of Renunciation
texts.description: Chapter 5 of the Bhagavad Gita: The wise see no difference between renunciation and selfless action — both lead to liberation.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 6: The Yoga of Meditation
texts.description: Chapter 6 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna teaches the practice of meditation: sit firm, control the mind, and fix it on the Self.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 7: Knowledge of the Absolute
texts.description: Chapter 7 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna reveals that all of existence is strung on him like pearls on a thread.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 8: The Way to the Supreme
texts.description: Chapter 8 of the Bhagavad Gita: At the moment of death, whoever thinks of me alone attains my nature.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 9: The Royal Secret
texts.description: Chapter 9 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna reveals the most secret teaching: he pervades the entire cosmos yet is not contained by it.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 10: Divine Glories
texts.description: Chapter 10 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna reveals himself as the source of all: among the Adityas, I am Vishnu; among lights, I am the radiant sun.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 11: The Vision of the Universal Form
texts.description: Chapter 11 of the Bhagavad Gita: Arjuna sees Krishna's cosmic form — all of reality in one infinite body — and is terrified.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 12: The Yoga of Devotion
texts.description: Chapter 12 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna describes the qualities of the ideal devotee: free from hatred, compassionate, without ego.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 13: The Field and the Knower
texts.description: Chapter 13 of the Bhagavad Gita: The body is the field; the soul is the knower of the field. Knowing this distinction is true knowledge.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 14: The Three Gunas
texts.description: Chapter 14 of the Bhagavad Gita: All of nature consists of three qualities: sattva (goodness), rajas (passion), tamas (ignorance).

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 15: The Supreme Person
texts.description: Chapter 15 of the Bhagavad Gita: The cosmos is an inverted tree rooted in heaven. Cut it down with the axe of detachment.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 16: The Divine and Demonic Natures
texts.description: Chapter 16 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna distinguishes the divine nature (fearlessness, purity, charity) from the demonic (hypocrisy, arrogance, anger).

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 17: The Three Divisions of Faith
texts.description: Chapter 17 of the Bhagavad Gita: Faith, food, sacrifice, austerity, and charity each come in three kinds — sattvic, rajasic, and tamasic.

texts.title: Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 18: Liberation Through Renunciation
texts.description: Chapter 18 of the Bhagavad Gita: Krishna's final teaching: surrender all actions to me. Abandon all dharmas. I shall liberate you from all sins.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 2: The Taming of Enkidu
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 2 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: Enkidu is civilized through love, learns the arts of man, and becomes Gilgamesh's companion.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 3: The Journey to the Cedar Forest
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 3 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: Gilgamesh and Enkidu prepare for their journey to fight the monster Humbaba.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 4: The Arrival at the Cedar Forest
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 4 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: They reach the Cedar Forest and Gilgamesh is frightened by dreams.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 5: The Fight with Humbaba
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 5 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: Gilgamesh and Enkidu defeat the guardian of the forest with the help of the sun god Shamash.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 6: Ishtar's Proposal
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 6 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: The goddess Ishtar proposes marriage to Gilgamesh; he rejects her. She sends the Bull of Heaven.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 7: The Death of Enkidu
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 7 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: Enkidu falls ill and dies, and Gilgamesh's heart is broken.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 8: The Funeral of Enkidu
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 8 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: Gilgamesh mourns Enkidu and commissions a magnificent funeral.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 9: The Search for Immortality
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 9 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: Grief-stricken, Gilgamesh sets out to find Utnapishtim, the only mortal granted eternal life.

texts.title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet 12: Enkidu's Ghost
texts.source: Standard Babylonian version; Nineveh library tablets
texts.description: Tablet 12 of the Epic of Gilgamesh: A later addition: Enkidu's spirit returns from the underworld to describe the fates of the dead.
